title: Interview Transcript
study: Social Integration and Academic Divergence: An Analysis of International Student Experiences at HKUST(GZ)

participant.name: Kassymkhan
participant.time_at_hkust_gz: Almost two semesters (just under one year)

background_and_first_impressions.question: Could you briefly introduce yourself — your nationality, program, year of study, and how long have you been at HKUST(GZ)?
background_and_first_impressions.answer: My name is Kassymkhan. I am Kazakh, from Semey, Kazakhstan. I study Artificial Intelligence and I am a first-year student. I have been at HKUST(GZ) for almost two semesters now — just under a year.

background_and_first_impressions.question: What was your first impression of student life at HKUST(GZ), especially socially?
background_and_first_impressions.answer: My first impression was quite interesting. When we first arrived, there was an orientation camp, so there was not much studying — I was mostly spending time with the guys and getting to know people. Once the semester properly started in September, things picked up, but the workload did not feel overwhelming at first. I did not study enough early on, and that caught up with me when midterms came. On a personal level, I was genuinely excited — the campus felt futuristic and beautiful, and I thought everyone around me was incredibly smart. Socially, I did not interact much with Chinese students at first; my time was mostly spent with other Kazakhstani students.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: How easy or difficult was it for you to make friends when you first arrived? Why?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: It was not that hard at first, partly because I came already knowing some people from Kazakhstan — we arrived together, so I had a familiar social circle from the start. I also got to know a few people from Hong Kong and China, including my roommate. I did not make a large number of friends, but I made some. However, around the middle of the first semester, I started feeling quite lonely. It crept up gradually rather than being something I felt from the very beginning.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Do you feel that you belong socially at HKUST(GZ)? Why or why not?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: I am not sure I fully belong here socially. The environment — especially among Chinese students who are academically very driven — feels somewhat distant from where I am. I do not feel like I fit in naturally, but I am genuinely trying to find my place and working towards that.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Who do you mostly spend time with on campus — students from your own background, Chinese students, or a mix? What shaped that pattern?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: I mostly spend time with students from my own background — predominantly people from Kazakhstan and Central Asia more broadly. Most Chinese students I encounter tend to be introverted and stay within their own social circles, so my social life has largely formed around people from similar backgrounds to mine.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Have you ever felt left out, disconnected, or excluded in social situations on campus? Can you describe a specific example?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: Yes, I did experience that — particularly during the first semester. Even among the people I was already familiar with, some had formed small tight-knit groups that I was not part of, which left me feeling excluded at times. It improved gradually over time, but during that period I did feel genuinely disconnected.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: How would you describe your interactions with Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: I only know a small number of Chinese students. Most of them tend to be introverted and prefer to communicate within their own groups rather than reaching out to international students. My main point of contact is my roommate, and occasionally one or two more outgoing Chinese students. Overall, my interaction with Chinese students has been quite limited.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: What makes it easier to connect with Chinese students, and what makes it harder?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: It is easier to connect when a Chinese student is outgoing and genuinely curious about cultural exchange — those individuals are approachable. When someone is more introverted or simply not motivated to engage, it becomes very difficult. Honestly, I do not think it is realistically possible to build many meaningful friendships with Chinese students here, because many of them are not particularly open to it.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: To what extent do language differences affect your social relationships here?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: Language differences play one of the most important roles. Students from Central Asia are not native English speakers either, but we actively try to communicate in English. The issue with many Chinese students is not necessarily that their English is poor — some have decent English — but that they lack the confidence to use it. They would rather stay silent than risk appearing uncertain or making mistakes. That reluctance to communicate is a real barrier, and it is compounded for those who are also naturally introverted.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: To what extent do cultural differences affect your comfort, confidence, or communication with others?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: [This question was not addressed in the interview session.]

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: Do you think students at HKUST(GZ) mix naturally across backgrounds, or do they mostly stay in separate groups? Why?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: I do not think students mix naturally here. Even within the international student community, Kazakhstani students have split into two or more separate groups. International students tend to socialise among themselves, and Chinese students among themselves. Genuine mixing across those lines does not happen very naturally.

academic_experience.question: Do you think your social experience at university has affected your academic life? How?
academic_experience.answer: Yes, my social experience has had a negative impact in some ways. Not having people around who could explain things or share how to study effectively meant I had to figure most things out on my own. If I had had access to that kind of peer support — particularly from Chinese students, who are often very academically skilled — I might have performed better.

academic_experience.question: Has your ability or inability to build friendships influenced your motivation, confidence, or study habits?
academic_experience.answer: The inability to form deep friendships did affect my motivation for a period — it was genuinely discouraging. That improved over time. In terms of study habits, not having a friend or study partner who could model effective approaches was a real gap. Chinese students tend to know how to study very efficiently, and not having access to that peer knowledge may have cost me academically.

academic_experience.question: How has your social experience affected your access to study groups, academic help, or useful information?
academic_experience.answer: Because I did not have many Chinese friends, I had no access to study groups. Even among Kazakhstani students, study groups did not really form. I studied on my own for most of the year. In terms of useful guidance, the friends I did have — including some second-year students — were not particularly motivated to share practical advice that could have helped me adapt better in my first year.

academic_experience.question: In group projects or collaborative assignments, have social or cultural dynamics affected your experience? Can you give an example?
academic_experience.answer: In assignments where groups were formed among international students — for example, in a sociology course involving presentations — the dynamics worked in my favour. International students tend to be more outgoing and more comfortable with presentations, which made those collaborations feel natural and productive.

academic_experience.question: Have you ever felt academically disadvantaged because you were not well connected socially? Why or why not?
academic_experience.answer: I am not entirely sure whether the disadvantage I experienced was primarily social or structural. Students who went through the Gaokao are academically extremely well-prepared — that system produces students who are genuinely exceptional in rigorous academic settings. That structural difference is probably the more significant factor in any academic gap I have noticed.

academic_experience.question: Do you think students with stronger social networks generally perform better academically here? What makes you say that?
academic_experience.answer: Not always — but in courses that require communication or collaboration, having a strong social network is definitely a meaningful advantage. In those contexts, who you know and how well you can work with others genuinely matters.

academic_experience.question: Have you noticed any difference in your performance between UCUG and UFUG courses? What do you think explains that difference?
academic_experience.answer: Yes. I performed noticeably worse in UFUG courses than in UCUG courses. My high school did not prepare me well for rigorous subject-specific content, so the fundamental courses were harder for me. Common core courses were more accessible, partly because they rely more on communication and English — areas where I feel relatively more confident than most Chinese students.

academic_experience.question: Do factors like language, class participation, or social connections affect your grades in UCUG and UFUG courses differently? How?
academic_experience.answer: Language probably affected my performance indirectly — not knowing Chinese meant I could not reach out to Chinese students who might have helped me. Class participation was not an issue; I actively participated in all my classes. In terms of social connections, they did help in UCUG courses, but they did not compensate for my gaps in the more content-heavy UFUG courses.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: What do you think are the main barriers preventing stronger integration between international and Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: The main barriers are language and the university's lack of active effort to promote integration. The institution does not do enough to bridge the gap between international and Chinese students — it largely leaves it to chance and individual initiative.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: Do you think the university does enough to help international students integrate socially and academically? Why or why not?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: I do not think the university does enough. They organise some activities for international students, but those efforts have not meaningfully improved my social life or academic experience. The friendships I have formed happened through my own initiative. Academically, the institutional support has also been minimal.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: What changes would you recommend to improve social integration and academic support for international students?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: I would suggest the university organise structured programmes designed to help students study more effectively and integrate better with Chinese students — initiatives that actively bring people together rather than leaving integration entirely to chance.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: Is there anything about the international student experience at HKUST(GZ) that people usually overlook or misunderstand?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: I think what people often overlook is that international students did not go through the same academic preparation as Chinese students. We are not built the same way academically. Some courses — especially fundamental ones — are significantly harder for us, and that struggle often goes unacknowledged by those around us.

end_note: Transcript cleaned and organised. Original audio recording retained for verification.
